pgrads.pgrad:
TRIGGER:
Step 0 — R0_base returned Gitmo/Ghana coverage and detainee lists but no explicit campaign name, hashtag, or petition title in Jan 2016 coverage.

KEEP:
Anchors and local actors: "OccupyGhana" / "Occupy Ghana", "New Patriotic Party" / "NPP", "Ghana", "January 2016", "Yemeni ex-detainees", "Gitmo" / "Guantanamo". Bridge clues retained: "online campaign", "hashtag", "petition", "e-petition", "social media protest".

ADD_OR_RESTORE:
Add site- and platform-focused cues and phrasing likely to surface named campaigns: site:ghanaweb.com OR site:joyonline.com OR site:change.org OR site:facebook.com/OccupyGhana OR site:twitter.com/OccupyGhana; include type cues and patterns: "Change.org petition", "online petition", "e-petition", "signed petition", "via Change.org", "Facebook post", "tweet", quoted campaign title patterns ("\"...\""), hashtag token indicator ("#*"); include alias forms: "Occupy Ghana", "OccupyGhana movement", and short-date anchors "Jan 2016" / "January 2016".

REMOVE_OR_AVOID:
Avoid generic Gitmo lists, timelines, and detainee biographies (e.g., "List of Yemeni detainees", individual detainee pages, "timeline of releases"); deprioritize international summary outlets and opinion pieces that mention the transfer without embedding local social posts or petition links; exclude broad keywords like "hunger strike", "policy analysis", "Guantanamo history".

QUERY_SHAPE:
Constrain to local news + petition platforms + OccupyGhana social handles and look for explicit campaign-name or hashtag strings. Pattern: (site:ghanaweb.com OR site:joyonline.com OR site:change.org OR site:facebook.com/OccupyGhana OR site:twitter.com/OccupyGhana) + ("OccupyGhana" OR "Occupy Ghana" OR "NPP") + (Guantanamo OR Gitmo OR "ex-detainees") + (petition OR "online petition" OR "Change.org" OR "e-petition" OR hashtag indicator "#") + ("January 2016" OR "Jan 2016") with phrase-match preference for quoted campaign titles or embedded tweet text.

WHY_THIS_MOVES_RETRIEVAL:
Narrowing to local news sites, petition platforms, and OccupyGhana social handles raises BM25 relevance for pages that reproduce petition titles, quoted tweets, or hashtag strings (the explicit evidence type missing from R0). Adding type cues ("Change.org", "petition", "Facebook post", "#") biases scoring toward named online campaigns; removing detainee lists and broad Gitmo pages reduces noise from high‑recall but low‑precision sources so the engine surfaces articles or embeds that explicitly show a campaign name or hashtag opposing the Jan 2016 transfer.

TRIGGER:
Narrow and re-weight the local retrieval focus to include additional Ghanaian news outlets and activist/NPP social pages, and require co-occurrence of event tokens with campaign tokens (petition/hashtag/embedded link) so results surface explicit named online campaigns or hashtag strings tied to the Jan 2016 transfer.

KEEP:
- Anchors: OccupyGhana, GhanaWeb, JoyOnline, Change.org, January 2016 transfer, Yemeni ex-detainees.
- Bridge clues to prioritize: "Change.org petition", "article quoting or embedding a tweet", "online petition", "embedded Facebook post", explicit campaign-name phrases.
- Avoid searching broad international summaries or detainee bios.

ADD_OR_RESTORE:
- Add Ghana-focused outlets and political actor pages: Citi Newsroom, Graphic Online, New Patriotic Party (NPP) social posts/pages.
- Add social platforms and embedded-link patterns as high-value sources: OccupyGhana Facebook/Twitter posts, NPP Facebook/Twitter, Change.org campaign pages, direct links (change.org/, facebook.com/, twitter.com/).
- Bridge clues to emphasize: "hashtag", "Facebook campaign", "tweet embedded", "press release linking to petition", quoted campaign title or petition URL.
- Type cues: require local-news article sentence or social post embedding a link/hashtag/quoted campaign name; prefer pages dated January 2016.

REMOVE_OR_AVOID:
- International outlets that only summarize the transfer without local campaign metadata.
- Lists/timelines of Guantanamo releases, detainee biographies, broad Gitmo policy analyses, generic protest coverage and opinion pieces lacking campaign links or hashtags.
- Generic searches that return wide Gitmo timelines rather than site-specific campaign mentions.

QUERY_SHAPE:
Constrain search space to Ghana news domains + Change.org + OccupyGhana/NPP social accounts; require co-occurrence of event tokens (Ghana OR "January 2016" OR Yemeni OR Guantanamo OR Gitmo OR "ex-detainees") with campaign tokens (petition OR "online petition" OR Change.org OR hashtag OR "#" OR "Facebook" OR "tweet" OR "press release" OR quoted phrase). Prefer hits that contain embedded-link patterns (change.org/, facebook.com/, twitter.com/) or quoted campaign names/hashtags and are dated around Jan 2016.

WHY_THIS_MOVES_RETRIEVAL:
Broadening anchor sites to include additional Ghanaian outlets and party/activist social pages while requiring event+campaign token co-occurrence focuses BM25 ranking on local reports or social posts that explicitly reproduce petition titles, URLs or hashtags. Excluding international summaries and detainee lists reduces noise so named online-campaign strings (petition title, hashtag, embedded link) rise in results. [[ ## completed ## ]]

TRIGGER:
Step 2 — narrow from general Ghana Gitmo coverage toward pages that reproduce named online campaign tokens (hashtags, petition titles, embedded social posts).

KEEP:
- Focus on Ghana-local coverage and actor anchors: OccupyGhana / "Occupy Ghana", New Patriotic Party (NPP), GhanaWeb, JoyOnline, Citi Newsroom, Graphic Online.
- Event/date anchors: Ghana transfer, Guantanamo / Gitmo, January 2016, Yemeni ex-detainees.
- Continue deprioritizing broad international retrospectives and detainee biographies.

ADD_OR_RESTORE:
- Add explicit campaign identifier tokens and link patterns to the retrieval target: hashtag markers (# or 'hashtag'), literal "Change.org", "petition", "e‑petition", "sign the petition", "Facebook campaign", "Twitter", "tweet", "via Twitter", "embed", "posted on Facebook".
- Add alias variants: "OccupyGhana" OR "Occupy Ghana" and common spellings of NPP.
- Prefer pages that quote or reproduce campaign names (quoted strings / embedded posts) or contain direct links (change.org, twitter.com, facebook.com) in body text.
- Bias toward Ghana news domains and activist social pages (explicit domain tokens: ghanaweb, joyonline, citinewsroom, graphic.com.gh, facebook.com/OccupyGhana, twitter.com/OccupyGhana).

REMOVE_OR_AVOID:
- Avoid reliance on general Gitmo timelines, lists of detainees, detainee biographies, historical analyses, and opinion pieces that lack campaign metadata.
- Downweight international summaries that omit local social-media embeds and any results that mention "protest" without campaign or link tokens.
- Avoid generic "protest" or "opposition" tokens unaccompanied by campaign identifiers.

QUERY_SHAPE:
- Require AND of three groups: (actor group) AND (event/date) AND (campaign identifier).
  - Actor group (OR): OccupyGhana OR "Occupy Ghana" OR NPP OR "New Patriotic Party"
  - Event/date (OR): Ghana OR Ghanaian OR "January 2016" OR 2016 OR Guantanamo OR Gitmo OR "Yemeni ex-detainees"
  - Campaign identifier (OR): hashtag OR "#" OR "Change.org" OR change.org OR petition OR "e‑petition" OR "sign the petition" OR "Facebook" OR "Twitter" OR "tweet" OR "via Twitter" OR "embed"
- Prefer documents containing quoted strings or link patterns (literal "http", "change.org", "twitter.com", "facebook.com") and boost results from Ghana-local domains.
- Match substrings for possible hashtag variants (allow token with leading '#') rather than requiring exact campaign name.

WHY_THIS_MOVES_RETRIEVAL:
Adding explicit campaign/link tokens (hashtag, Change.org, petition, Facebook/Twitter, quoted strings, link patterns) and boosting Ghana-local domains shifts BM25 scoring away from generic Gitmo coverage and detainee biographies toward articles or posts that reproduce online-campaign identifiers. Requiring the conjunction of actor + event + campaign identifier increases precision for pages that actually name or embed the online protest (hashtags, petition titles, social-post embeds) rather than pages that merely report the transfer. This raises the rank of local news items or activist social pages that are likely to contain the alternate campaign name.

TRIGGER:
Step 3 local update when current retrieval_focus targets contemporary news/social‑media coverage of the January 2016 Ghana transfer but search results lack a quoted campaign label (hashtag, petition title, or named online campaign) tied to OccupyGhana or NPP.

KEEP:
- Anchors (preserve order): OccupyGhana / Occupy Ghana; New Patriotic Party (NPP); Ghana; January 2016 transfer; Guantanamo / Gitmo; Yemeni ex-detainees; news articles and social media excerpts.
- Bridge clues still prioritized: quoted hashtag; online petition; e‑petition; Facebook campaign; Twitter campaign or tweet embed; named online protest; phrases like "petition to" or "hashtag" in article; coverage that links to campaign pages.

ADD_OR_RESTORE:
- Aliases and variant spellings: "Occupy Ghana", "OccupyGhana", "New Patriotic Party", "NPP".
- Medium/type cues to bias toward social coverage: Twitter, Facebook, "hashtag", "online petition", "e‑petition", "Change.org", "Avaaz", "petition site", "Facebook page", "tweet embed".
- Quoted‑label triggers and phrase matches: literal markers such as 'hashtag', 'petition', 'campaign', 'online campaign', 'petition titled', 'called for return', 'opposed the transfer', 'called the deal "secretive"' — include exact-phrase matching behavior (prefer results that contain quoted tokens).
- Hashtag-wildcard behavior: allow substring/wildcard hashtag patterns (e.g., patterns matching "#*ghana*", "#*gitmo*", "#*occupy*") rather than relying on an exact token.
- Time/event anchors reinforced: "January 2016", "2016 transfer", "transfer to Ghana", "Gitmo detainees Ghana".
- Retrieval bias: prefer snippets that embed or quote a short label (one token or short phrase) — e.g., a quoted hashtag or petition title presented inline in the article.

REMOVE_OR_AVOID:
- Noisy distractors to downweight: lists of Guantanamo detainees, individual detainee biographies, general Gitmo history/timelines, unrelated pieces titled "Kamunting", "Gitmo on the Platte", "Yemeni Revolution", non‑campaign blog posts, and broad background analysis that lack social‑media embeds or quoted campaign labels.
- Avoid long encyclopedia-style lists and document collections that score well on detainee names but do not contain social‑media campaign text.

QUERY_SHAPE:
Combine actor anchors + event/time tokens + medium/type cues + quoted‑label and hashtag‑pattern matching. Behaviorally: prioritize documents containing (OccupyGhana OR "Occupy Ghana" OR NPP OR "New Patriotic Party") AND (Ghana OR Guantanamo OR Gitmo) AND (January 2016 OR 2016 OR "transfer to Ghana") AND (hashtag OR "online petition" OR "e‑petition" OR Twitter OR Facebook OR Change.org OR Avaaz) with allowance for substring hashtag matches and exact‑phrase quote matches (articles that embed/quote a short token or campaign title).

WHY_THIS_MOVES_RETRIEVAL:
- Adding social‑media medium cues and platform names (Twitter, Facebook, Change.org, "hashtag") steers BM25 toward news pieces that include embedded tweets, petition links, or quoted campaign names rather than general reportage or detainee lists.
- Wildcard/substring hashtag matching and explicit quoted‑label triggers increase recall for articles that reproduce a campaign token under variable spellings while still prioritizing short, high‑signal labels.
- Reinforcing the January 2016 + transfer + OccupyGhana/NPP anchor combination narrows time/event context, and explicitly downweighting detainee lists removes high‑scoring but irrelevant documents, improving chance of surfacing a named online protest (hashtag or petition title) in news or social embeds.

pgrads.pgrad:
TRIGGER:
focus-local-Ghana-news + "online/hashtag/petition" signals

KEEP:
- Anchors: OccupyGhana; New Patriotic Party (NPP); Ghana; January 2016 transfer; Yemeni ex-detainees; Guantanamo/Gitmo
- Retrieval focus wording: "news or social-media mentions (coverage of the Jan 2016 Ghana transfer) that explicitly name an online protest, campaign title, or hashtag"
- Bridge clues to retain: "online campaign", "hashtag", "e-petition", "social media protest", "embedded tweet/Facebook post reproduced in reporting"

ADD_OR_RESTORE:
- Local outlet anchors: GhanaWeb, MyJoyOnline / JoyOnline, Citi News / Citi FM, Graphic Online
- Online-campaign tokens and aliases: "hashtag", "online petition", "e-petition", "Change.org", "via Twitter", "Facebook post", "embedded tweet", "petition titled", "campaign called"
- Date/type coupling: explicit "January 2016" or "Jan 2016" paired with "Ghana" + "Guantanamo"/"Gitmo"
- Query intent signals: look for quoted campaign titles or literal #hashtags inside article copy, and for phrases like "online petition" or "took to social media" near transfer coverage

REMOVE_OR_AVOID:
- Noisy anchors: detainee lists pages, individual detainee biographies (Obaidullah, Inayatullah), global Gitmo timelines, Guantanamo hunger strike pages
- Distractors: international summaries that omit local campaign details, opinion pieces on US policy that don't reproduce local social posts, generic release/transfer timeline lists

QUERY_SHAPE:
Bias retrieval toward Ghana local news articles and in-article social-media reproductions by combining:
- local outlet anchors (GhanaWeb, JoyOnline, Citi, Graphic) AND
- campaign-type tokens ("hashtag", "online petition", "e-petition", "Change.org", "embedded tweet", "Facebook post") AND
- event anchors ("Guantanamo" OR "Gitmo") + date anchor ("January 2016" OR "Jan 2016") and "OccupyGhana" or "NPP"
Prefer results where campaign-type tokens occur in the same sentence/paragraph as the transfer coverage (quoted campaign title or visible #token), and deprioritize pages dominated by detainee lists or timeline tables.

WHY_THIS_MOVES_RETRIEVAL:
- BM25-style matching benefits from adding concrete local outlet names and explicit campaign tokens; those raise term overlap with articles that reproduce or quote an online protest label or hashtag.
- Coupling date ("Jan 2016") and event tokens ("Guantanamo"/"Gitmo") narrows to contemporaneous local reporting rather than global timelines.
- Removing detainee-list and biography anchors reduces high-scoring but irrelevant pages that share "Guantanamo" vocabulary but lack social-media campaign mentions.
- Favoring in-paragraph co-occurrence of "hashtag"/"online petition" with the transfer story increases chances of surfacing a quoted campaign name or hashtag in Ghana reporting rather than generic Gitmo summaries.

TRIGGER:
Add petition-platform and social-account sources because local-Ghana articles alone have no reproduced hashtag or petition title; broaden retrieval toward Change.org / OccupyGhana social posts and petition pages tied to the Jan 2016 transfer.

KEEP:
- Ghana-focused anchors: GhanaWeb, JoyOnline / MyJoyOnline, Citi News / Citi FM, Graphic Online
- Core event anchors: OccupyGhana, New Patriotic Party (NPP), January 2016 transfer, Yemeni ex-detainees
- Bridge clues to preserve: article language like "online petition", "e-petition", quoted hashtag(s), "embedded tweet" or "embedded Facebook post", phrases where "online" or "petition" co-occur with "return" or "called for return"

ADD_OR_RESTORE:
- Petition and social anchors: Change.org, Facebook (OccupyGhana page), Twitter (OccupyGhana / local activists), direct petition-title patterns
- Bridge clues to surface: "via Change.org" or full Change.org petition URLs reproduced in articles; quoted petition titles; visible hashtags (e.g., "#...") embedded in article text or screenshots; Facebook post text or Twitter thread excerpts dated Jan 2016
- Type cues: search for "petition title", "Change.org petition", "online campaign", "hashtag", "e-petition", "Facebook post", "embedded tweet"
- Inspection behavior: open linked petition pages and social posts to read the petition title or the first tweet text (not only article summaries)

REMOVE_OR_AVOID:
- International Gitmo timelines, detainee biographical pages, lists of released detainees
- Opinion pieces or diplomatic summaries that omit local campaign details
- Generic protest coverage unrelated to the January 2016 Ghana transfer

QUERY_SHAPE:
Constrain and expand simultaneously: keep site-limited Ghana news checks but add site-targeted lookups and social/petition inspections. Example behavioral recipe: include site filters for GhanaWeb/JoyOnline AND also query Change.org and OccupyGhana social handles; combine event tokens ("Guantanamo" OR "Gitmo") + location token ("Ghana") + date token ("January 2016" / "2016") + type cues ("petition" OR "hashtag" OR "online" OR "via Change.org" OR "embedded tweet" OR "Facebook post"); prioritize pages that reproduce a petition title, a Change.org URL, or an embedded social-media post rather than summaries. After finding candidate pages, open petition/social links and extract the campaign/petition title or hashtag string.

WHY_THIS_MOVES_RETRIEVAL:
Local news articles may report opposition but often omit the original online campaign label; adding Change.org and OccupyGhana social accounts plus explicit "via Change.org"/"hashtag"/"petition title" cues shifts retrieval from secondary summaries to primary petition pages and embedded social posts where the campaign name or hashtag is most likely to appear. This increases chances of surfacing a quoted campaign title or hashtag tied to the Jan 2016 transfer.

TRIGGER:
Locate an explicit online campaign name, petition title, hashtag, or direct Change.org/Facebook/Twitter link used by OccupyGhana or local actors to oppose Ghana's January 2016 acceptance of Yemeni ex‑detainees from Guantanamo.

KEEP:
- Focus on the same small set of high‑yield local sources and activist pages: OccupyGhana, GhanaWeb, JoyOnline, Change.org.
- Maintain event anchors and context: "January 2016 transfer", "Ghana", "Yemeni ex‑detainees", "Guantanamo"/"Gitmo".
- Preserve bridge clues: mentions of a Change.org petition title/URL in news copy, article text quoting or embedding a tweet with a hashtag, the phrase "online petition" or "e‑petition", embedded Facebook posts or links to OccupyGhana.
- Keep excluding broad international summaries and detainee biography pages as lower priority.

ADD_OR_RESTORE:
- Constrain to Ghana‑focused domains and OccupyGhana social accounts (explicitly target GhanaWeb, JoyOnline, Citi/Graphic Online, Change.org, OccupyGhana Facebook/Twitter) and prioritize pages that include embedded social posts or direct petition links.
- Prioritize literal campaign tokens and URL patterns: "Change.org", "change.org/p/", "petition", "sign the petition", "hashtag", "use #", "via Change.org", quoted campaign phrases (e.g., "Bring them back", "Stop the transfer", "Return the detainees"), and obvious aliases ("Occupy Ghana", "OccupyGhana", "NPP").
- Add date and event co‑occurrence signals: require proximity of campaign tokens to "January 2016" or "Jan 2016" and "Ghana" + ("Gitmo" OR "Guantanamo" OR "ex‑detainees" OR "Yemeni") to boost BM25 overlap.
- Prefer text that reproduces names/phrases verbatim (quoted strings) or contains direct links/embeds to social posts or Change.org pages.

REMOVE_OR_AVOID:
- De‑prioritize international outlets or summaries that mention the transfer without local campaign metadata.
- Avoid detainee biography pages, lists/timelines of Guantanamo releases, and general Gitmo policy/opinion pieces that lack petition/hashtag/link evidence.
- Do not match on broad tokens alone (e.g., "protest", "Guantanamo") unless paired with campaign tokens or site filters.
- Avoid sources that only report statements by officials without linking to an online campaign (press conferences lacking embeds/links).

QUERY_SHAPE:
Use a site‑constrained, phrase‑and‑URL‑pattern oriented search: combine Ghana news/social domains + event terms (Ghana AND (Guantanamo OR Gitmo OR "ex‑detainees" OR Yemeni) AND date window Jan 2016) with campaign markers (Change.org OR "change.org/p/" OR petition OR "sign the petition" OR hashtag patterns "use #" OR quoted phrase matches). Prefer matches that include embedded social post indicators or literal links/quoted campaign names rather than broad topical matches.

WHY_THIS_MOVES_RETRIEVAL:
Narrowing to Ghana domains and OccupyGhana social pages while demanding literal campaign tokens, URL patterns, quoted phrases, and date proximity increases BM25 overlap with pages that actually reproduce a petition title, hashtag, or campaign name. Simultaneously excluding broad Gitmo summaries, detainee bios, and opinion pieces reduces high‑score false positives that lack the explicit online campaign metadata needed to answer the step.

TRIGGER:
narrow-to-quoted-campaign-identifiers-JAN2016

KEEP:
OccupyGhana / Occupy Ghana; New Patriotic Party (NPP); Ghana news outlets (GhanaWeb, JoyOnline, Citi Newsroom, Graphic Online); January 2016 transfer; Guantanamo / Gitmo; local activist social media (Facebook, Twitter).

ADD_OR_RESTORE:
Require evidence tokens that look like reproduced campaign identifiers: quoted hashtags or tokens ("#..."), exact petition titles, Change.org link patterns ("change.org", "petition"), phrases like "petition to", "signed a petition", "Facebook campaign", "tweet", "tweet embed", "posted on Facebook", "online campaign", "e‑petition", "campaign name", "named online protest". Prioritize results that include quotation marks, #tokens, URLs or embedded social posts alongside Ghana + Gitmo + 2016.

REMOVE_OR_AVOID:
Broad Gitmo background or biography material: lists of detainees, individual detainee biographies, historical analyses of Guantanamo releases, general timelines, international summaries without local campaign detail, opinion pieces or blogs that lack quoted hashtags/links, non‑campaign mentions.

QUERY_SHAPE:
Actor anchors (OccupyGhana OR "Occupy Ghana" OR NPP OR site:ghanaweb.com OR site:joyonline.com) + medium tokens (hashtag OR "#"+substring OR "Change.org" OR petition OR "e‑petition" OR "Facebook" OR "Twitter" OR "tweet" OR "posted on Facebook") + event/date tokens (Ghana OR Guantanamo OR Gitmo) + date filter (January 2016 OR 2016). Prefer matches where medium tokens appear as quoted strings, #tokens, or URLs.

WHY_THIS_MOVES_RETRIEVAL:
Shifts scoring from broad Ghana/Gitmo reportage toward items that explicitly reproduce an online campaign identifier (hashtag, petition title, Change.org link, embedded tweet/Facebook post). By requiring quoted/URL/ #token patterns and deprioritizing detainee lists and background analyses, BM25 will favor local articles or social posts that explicitly name or show the online protest, producing the campaign name/hashtag needed to answer the question.

TRIGGER:
Shift focus from actor-centered news coverage to actor+online-campaign identifier signals for the Jan 2016 Ghana Gitmo transfer (step 4).

KEEP:
- Anchors: OccupyGhana / "Occupy Ghana", New Patriotic Party (NPP), Ghana, Guantanamo / Gitmo, Yemeni ex-detainees, January 2016 transfer.
- Preservation of source types: contemporary news articles and embedded social‑media excerpts.
- Bridge clues to retain: quoted hashtag, online petition, Facebook/Twitter campaign mentions, phrase like "petition to" or "hashtag" in copy.

ADD_OR_RESTORE:
- Alias and token cues: "OccupyGhana" OR "Occupy Ghana" OR Occupy_Ghana; "NPP" OR "New Patriotic Party".
- Medium/type cues: hashtag, "#", "e‑petition", "online petition", "petition titled", "Facebook campaign", "Twitter campaign", "tweet", "embed", "signed", "campaign".
- Labeling verbs/phrases that signal a campaign name: "called", "dubbed", "described as", "labelled", "titled", "under the hashtag", "using the hashtag".
- Date variants: "January 2016", "Jan 2016", "2016".
- Substring/anchor patterns for BM25 matching on tags: allow "#*" or substring matches around "Gitmo", "Guant" (e.g., "#Gitmo", "#GitmoGhana"), and short token patterns likely to appear as campaign identifiers.

REMOVE_OR_AVOID:
- Lists of Guantanamo detainees, individual detainee biographies, long timelines of Gitmo releases.
- Unrelated topics: Yemeni Revolution, Kamunting, "Gitmo on the Platte", historical background pieces not linking to the Ghana transfer.
- Generic opinion pieces or non‑campaign blog posts that lack embedded social posts or quoted campaign labels.

QUERY_SHAPE:
Behavioral query shape (not a final query): combine actor aliases + event/location + year + medium/label tokens. Compact token groups with ORs and substring hashtag matching, e.g.
(OccupyGhana OR "Occupy Ghana" OR NPP) + (Ghana OR Guantanamo OR Gitmo) + (2016 OR "Jan 2016") + (hashtag OR "#" OR "e‑petition" OR "petition titled" OR Facebook OR Twitter OR tweet OR "called" OR "dubbed").

WHY_THIS_MOVES_RETRIEVAL:
- Adding short token cues (hashtag, e‑petition, "petition titled", Facebook/Twitter, labeling verbs) boosts BM25 weighting for documents that quote or reproduce campaign identifiers (the exact short labels/news-embedded tokens we need).
- Including actor aliases and strict date/location terms keeps matches anchored to the Ghana Jan 2016 transfer and OccupyGhana/NPP commentary.
- Substring/hashtag pattern matching increases recall for variant tag spellings without pulling long, unrelated Gitmo background.
- Removing detainee-list and historical-noise tokens prevents high-scoring but irrelevant documents (biographies, timelines) from crowding out short-labeled campaign mentions.

question: Kunihiko Tanaka designed the characters for which video game developed by Monolith Soft ?
summary_1:
Answer: Xenoblade Chronicles X — Kunihiko Tanaka is credited as a character designer for Xenoblade Chronicles X, a role-playing game developed by Monolith Soft.

Key facts / entities:
- Kunihiko Tanaka | Japanese manga artist & character designer; born August 12, 1970; credited on character design for Xenogears and Xenoblade Chronicles X; also did character-concept work for the anime Key the Metal Idol; wrote the Ruin Explorers manga.
- Xenoblade Chronicles X | Title in the Xeno series; developed by Monolith Soft; published by Nintendo; (bridge target for verification: Xenoblade Chronicles X page / credits).
- Monolith Soft | Japanese game developer founded 1999 by Tetsuya Takahashi; known for Xenosaga and the Xenoblade series; former Square staff (Xenogears) joined the studio.
- Related entities / bridge clues for follow-up retrieval: Xenogears (Square-developed; Tanaka lead character designer), Xenosaga (Monolith Soft series; KOS-MOS character), Tetsuya Takahashi (Monolith Soft founder), list of Monolith Soft-developed games, and Kunihiko Tanaka credits page (to confirm which characters he designed on Xenoblade Chronicles X).

Missing/verify:
- Confirm the specific character credits and on which Monolith Soft titles beyond Xenoblade Chronicles X Tanaka is listed (check game credits / Kunihiko Tanaka filmography).

question: Who were the writers of an American television series based on the DC Comics character Superman, which starred an American actor known for his television roles that include Fox Crane on the NBC daytime soap opera "Passions"?
summary_1:
- TV series | Smallville — American television series based on the DC Comics character Superman; aired 2001–2011; starred Justin Hartley as Oliver Queen (Hartley is also known for Fox Crane on the NBC soap Passions).
- Primary writers/creators | Alfred Gough and Miles Millar — credited as the creators/writers and executive producers who developed Smallville.
- Actor | Justin Hartley — known roles include Fox Crane on Passions (2002–2006) and Oliver Queen on Smallville (2006–2011).
- Additional writing staff (not named in passages) | Numerous other writers/producers contributed across the series run (e.g., Kelly Souders, Brian Peterson, Todd Slavkin, Darren Swimmer among others) — see "Smallville" writing credits or "List of Smallville episodes" for full credits.
- Missing/for follow-up | The provided passages mention Justin Hartley but do not list Smallville writers; relevant items to retrieve next: "Alfred Gough", "Miles Millar", "Smallville (TV series) writing credits", and "List of Smallville episodes/writers".

pgrads.pgrad:
TRIGGER:
When next retrieving evidence for step 0 (confirming Smallville's writers) and current results are dominated by episode pages or individual staff biographies instead of a show-level credits page.

KEEP:
Smallville; writing credits; Alfred Gough; Miles Millar; List of Smallville episodes; Justin Hartley; Oliver Queen; Fox Crane; Passions.

ADD_OR_RESTORE:
Target-type cues and phrases: "Smallville (TV series)", "created by", "developed by", "credited as creators", "writing credits", "series main article", "episode list / consolidated credits", date range "2001–2011", "creator bios", "official press release" — restore focus on show-level sources and the two creator names as primary anchors.

REMOVE_OR_AVOID:
Avoid episode-specific titles (Pilot, Finale, Tempest, Reckoning), long enumerations of secondary writers (Kelly Souders, Brian Peterson, Todd Slavkin, Darren Swimmer), individual person profile pages when they lack series-overview context, fan wikis, actor filmography pages, and unrelated DC property or news pages.

QUERY_SHAPE:
Prefer short, show-level retrieval patterns: show title + TV-series/type cue + credit phrase + creator names + consolidated-credits source. (e.g., prioritize "Smallville (TV series)" main article, "List of Smallville episodes" credit summary, and Alfred Gough / Miles Millar bios; include "created by"/"writing credits" and optionally "2001–2011" and Justin Hartley as a casting bridge; do not enumerate many secondary writer names or episode titles.)

WHY_THIS_MOVES_RETRIEVAL:
BM25 favors documents containing exact show-title + credit phrases; emphasizing "created by"/"writing credits" and the episode-list or series main article raises scores of authoritative series-level pages and creator bios while removing episode names and many person names reduces hits on episode pages and individual staff profiles. Including Justin Hartley as a bridge preserves the actor-to-series link without dragging retrieval toward actor filmographies or plot-focused episode pages. This refocuses retrieval from scattered person/episode pages to consolidated series-level credit sources.

TRIGGER:
Step 1 — current results are name-heavy and include many episode/person pages while the actor link (Justin Hartley) appears; move toward concise show-level creator credits.

KEEP:
- Anchors: Smallville (TV series / "Smallville"), List of Smallville episodes, Alfred Gough, Miles Millar, Justin Hartley
- Bridge clues: American television series based on Superman; starred Justin Hartley; Oliver Queen; Fox Crane; Passions; creators / primary series creators; series credits

ADD_OR_RESTORE:
- Boost phrases: "created by", "developed by", "writing credits"
- Restore authoritative source types: Smallville main article (series overview with credits), consolidated episode-list credit summary, creator biographies for Alfred Gough and Miles Millar, industry press pieces (Variety, Hollywood Reporter), official press release / network credits
- Alias/date cues to favor: "Smallville (2001–2011)", "Smallville — created by Alfred Gough and Miles Millar"
- Keep Justin Hartley as a light-weight bridge token to confirm casting linkage

REMOVE_OR_AVOID:
- Individual episode pages (Pilot, Finale, Tempest, Reckoning) and their plot-heavy anchors
- Long enumerations/person pages for many secondary writers (Kelly Souders, Brian Peterson, Todd Slavkin, Darren Swimmer, etc.)
- Fan wikis, character analyses, actor filmographies that do not state series-level credits
- Unrelated DC project pages or tangential news items

QUERY_SHAPE:
Concise show-plus-creators pattern (behavioral template, not a final query): [Smallville | "Smallville (2001–2011)"] + ("created by" OR "developed by" OR "writing credits") + (Alfred Gough) + (Miles Millar) + (List of Smallville episodes OR Smallville main article) [+ optional: Justin Hartley as bridge]

WHY_THIS_MOVES_RETRIEVAL:
Shifts BM25 weight toward series-level credit statements and creator bios by promoting exact cue phrases ("created by"/"developed by") and authoritative page types, while demoting episode-level and many-person pages. This narrows results from scattered episode/person documents to the Smallville main/credits pages and creator profiles where Alfred Gough and Miles Millar are explicitly named as the show's creators/writers, keeping Justin Hartley only as a casting-bridge token.

TRIGGER:
On step 2, after an initial broad/name-heavy pass returns many individual writer/episode pages, tighten the retrieval instruction to favor series-level creation/credits pages that name the show's creators.

KEEP:
Smallville; Alfred Gough; Miles Millar; List of Smallville episodes; Justin Hartley / Fox Crane / Passions; phrases pointing to series-level credits.

ADD_OR_RESTORE:
Explicitly add show-level credit anchors and type/date cues: "created by" OR "developed by" OR "writers"; "Smallville (TV series) 2001–2011"; "writing credits" / "series credits"; authoritative source cues (e.g., Variety, Hollywood Reporter, official credits page). Restore the two creator names as primary anchor terms alongside the show title rather than as one item among many writers.

REMOVE_OR_AVOID:
Avoid long enumerations of episodic writer names and individual episode pages (Pilot, Tempest, Finale, etc.), individual staff/person profile pages for secondary writers, fan wikis and character pages, unrelated DC projects or pilot pages that surface by name overlap. De-emphasize full episode lists of contributors as separate retrieval targets.

QUERY_SHAPE:
Concise show-plus-creators form: [Smallville (TV series) 2001–2011] + [created by | developed by | writers | writing credits] + [Alfred Gough] + [Miles Millar] + source-type cues (List of Smallville episodes, official credits, industry press). Optionally include actor bridge terms (Justin Hartley, Fox Crane, Passions) to tie the actor mention to the series-level documents.

WHY_THIS_MOVES_RETRIEVAL:
Shifts BM25 weight onto high-value tokens (show title, "created by", creator names, credit/list pages, date/type cues) so authoritative series-level pages rank higher and term dilution from many peripheral writer/episode names is reduced. Including actor bridge terms preserves the actor→show link without dragging in many person-specific or fan pages. The result surfaces consolidated credit statements (main article, episode-list credits, industry press) that explicitly name the creators.

TRIGGER:
Question mentions Justin Hartley / Fox Crane / Passions plus a Superman-based American TV series (Smallville) and asks who wrote or created that series.

KEEP:
- Show-level anchors: "Smallville", "Smallville (TV series)"
- Primary creator names: "Alfred Gough", "Miles Millar"
- Bridge clues linking actor to show: "Justin Hartley", "Fox Crane", "Passions", "Oliver Queen"
- Expected evidence type: "series article", "List of Smallville episodes" with "writing credits"
- Goal: prefer consolidated pages that state creators/writers rather than many individual person or episode pages

ADD_OR_RESTORE:
- BM25-friendly writer/creator cues: "creators", "created by", "writers", "writing credits"
- Combined anchor phrases: "Smallville creators", "Smallville writers", "Smallville writing credits"
- Creator-focused phrases: "Alfred Gough Smallville", "Miles Millar Smallville", "Alfred Gough Miles Millar"
- Type/date cues to strengthen series-level matches: "Smallville (2001–2011)", "series article", "episode list"
- Bio/credit targets: "Alfred Gough biography", "Miles Millar biography", "official credits", "executive producers"
- Noise-removal anchors to reprioritize: deprioritize actor-centric tokens unless paired with show-level cues

REMOVE_OR_AVOID:
- Individual episode pages and episode titles (e.g., "Pilot", "Tempest")
- Character pages and cast-centric pages (e.g., "Chloe Sullivan", full cast lists)
- Actor-only queries that surface many person pages (e.g., "Justin Hartley biography" without "Smallville")
- Other DC or pilot projects that share names (e.g., "Aquaman (TV pilot)")
- Long, name-heavy lists of many writers as separate query terms

QUERY_SHAPE:
Short, show-centered noun phrases and biographic pairings that favor series-level documents — examples of the intended shape (behavioral templates, not final queries):
- [Smallville] + (creators | created by | writers | writing credits | "List of Smallville episodes")
- [Alfred Gough] + [Smallville] or [Miles Millar] + [Smallville] (bio/credits/type cue)
- [Smallville] + (2001–2011 | series article | episode list | writing credits)
Prefer 2–4 token fragments with explicit type cues rather than long enumerations of individual writer names.

WHY_THIS_MOVES_RETRIEVAL:
- Using concise show + role/type phrases (e.g., "Smallville" + "creators"/"writing credits") increases BM25 overlap with authoritative pages (series article, episode list, creator bios) that explicitly state who created/wrote the series.
- Including the two creator names as paired anchors (Alfred Gough, Miles Millar) biases ranking toward creator biographies and consolidated credit lists rather than scattering results across many episode or cast pages.
- Adding date and type cues (2001–2011, "episode list", "series article") further distinguishes series-level sources from noisy episode- or character-level documents.
- Explicitly avoiding episode titles, character pages, actor-only queries, and other DC projects reduces retrieval of irrelevant documents that previously dominated the result set.

max_info.why_largest: This transition makes the biggest pivot from a long, name- and episode-heavy query that returns many person/episode pages to a concise, show-focused retrieval targeting authoritative series-level sources (Smallville main article, episode list, and creator bios). It removes noisy episode/person anchors, corrects noisy-entity distractions, and changes the query/evidence family shape to prioritize creator-level writing credits; subsequent segments are only minor refinements of this reframing.

question: Between G. K. Chesterton and Henry Green, who had more diverse job experiences?
summary_1:
Answer: Cannot determine from the provided passages — Henry Green is missing; available passages mention G. K. Chesterton (author) and a different person, Joseph Henry Chesterton (plant collector).

Key facts from passages:
- G. K. Chesterton | Author of Orthodoxy (1908); Orthodoxy described as a classic of Christian apologetics and a companion to his earlier Heretics — indicates Chesterton's role as a writer and Christian apologist (literary/theological work dated 1908).
- Joseph Henry Chesterton (not Henry Green) | c.1840–26 Jan 1883; plant collector sent by James Veitch & Sons to search for orchids in South America — single documented occupational role (botanical/plant-collecting).
- Missing entity (bridge clue) | "Henry Green" (no passage provided). To answer the question you need Henry Green’s occupations/job history (e.g., full name, dates, roles/occupations).

Suggested next retrievals (bridge queries) to resolve the comparison:
- "Henry Green occupations" or "Henry Green biography" (to get jobs/roles).
- "G. K. Chesterton occupations" or "G. K. Chesterton biography" (to list all his professional roles beyond author/apologist).
- Clarify potential name confusion: "Henry Chesterton vs Henry Green" or "Joseph Henry Chesterton plant collector" to avoid conflating individuals.

QUERY_SHAPE:
Construct a single-entity, exact-match retrieval step: prioritize queries that explicitly pair the pen name with the legal name and occupation cues — e.g., exact-phrase + role tokens: ("Henry Vincent Yorke" OR "Henry Green (novelist)") AND ("biography" OR "infobox" OR "occupations" OR "career") with an optional novel-title anchor ("Party Going") — do not combine multiple target entities in this step; prefer lead/infobox results.

WHY_THIS_MOVES_RETRIEVAL:
Specifying the canonical name + pen-name mapping and occupation/infobox cues increases BM25 relevance for the novelist's encyclopedia/biography lead and suppresses noisy disambiguation and other-Henry hits. Anchoring with a known work title further biases results to the correct Henry Green page. Restricting to single-entity, lead/infobox-style evidence yields a concise occupations list needed to compare job diversity while avoiding confusion with Joseph Henry Chesterton.

- Short entity refinement for Chesterton after Henry resolved: [G. K. Chesterton] + [Orthodoxy 1908] + [infobox | occupations | essayist | journalist | theologian].
- Disambiguation-cleanup mini-query: [Joseph Henry Chesterton] + [plant collector] + [1840 OR 1883] + [James Veitch & Sons] as a negative/clarifying anchor.

WHY_THIS_MOVES_RETRIEVAL:

pgrads.pgrad:
TRIGGER:
When initial multi-target retrieval returns many unrelated "Henry" hits or a disambiguation page and the passages include "Joseph Henry Chesterton" but not "Henry Green" (i.e., target pen-name is missing or confused with other Henrys).

KEEP:
- Anchor to existing knowns: G. K. Chesterton present as an author/apologist; Joseph Henry Chesterton present as a 19th‑century plant collector (to disambiguate).
- Goal: obtain a concise biographic identity for the missing target needed for a job-role comparison.
- Local step order: first confirm identity/pen‑name mapping, then capture a one‑line occupation/date label.

ADD_OR_RESTORE:
- Force exact-entity identity evidence: pen‑name → legal name mapping (e.g., "Henry Green" = "Henry Vincent Yorke") using lead/infobox phrasing like "born Henry Vincent Yorke" or "Henry Green (born Henry Vincent Yorke; 1905–1973) was an English novelist".
- Type cues and one-line role label: include "novelist", "English novelist", notable title anchor ("Party Going") as secondary confirmers.
- Date anchors to strengthen BM25 signal (e.g., 1905–1973) so results return concise encyclopedic leads.
- Restrict scope to lead paragraph / infobox lines or one‑sentence biography snippets (identity + primary role + dates).

REMOVE_OR_AVOID:
- Avoid broad, combined multi-entity occupation queries that conflate targets (e.g., queries that mix Chesterton + Green in one string).
- Avoid disambiguation-list pages and generic "Henry Green" lists that surface many unrelated Henrys.
- Avoid botanical / 19th‑century plant‑collector documents tied to "Joseph Henry Chesterton" and other common Henrys (Henry James, Henry F. Pringle, etc.).
- Avoid noisy terms like just "occupations" without an exact-entity anchor.

QUERY_SHAPE:
Use an exact-entity identity template that prioritizes pen‑name mapping + type cue + date + host of lead text. Example shape patterns (behavioral templates, not final combined query):
- exact-match pen‑name + equals/mapping phrasing: '"Henry Green" = "Henry Vincent Yorke"' or '“Henry Green” "born Henry Vincent Yorke"'
- lead-sentence fetch shape: '("Henry Green" OR "Henry Vincent Yorke") AND (novelist OR "English novelist") AND (1905 OR 1973) + lead/infobox'
- include a known work as a verifier: '"Henry Green" "Party Going" "novelist" lead'
Always bias for short encyclopedic lead or infobox snippets rather than full biographies.

WHY_THIS_MOVES_RETRIEVAL:
Narrowing to an exact pen‑name → legal‑name mapping with occupation and date anchors increases BM25 overlap on concise lead sentences, eliminating distractions from other Henrys and botanical Joseph Chesterton pages. That produces the single-line identity/role needed to compare job diversity (e.g., "novelist" + life dates) without fetching long, noisy occupation lists.

TRIGGER:
When the question asks to compare job/occupation diversity and the passages lack any clear identity or occupations for "Henry Green" (pen name ambiguous or missing), while G. K. Chesterton is present.

KEEP:
- Keep the local aim to confirm the pen-name mapping (Henry Green ↔ Henry Vincent Yorke).
- Keep the constraint to return a short encyclopedic lead/infobox-style line (identity + primary role label + life dates).
- Keep anchors that disambiguate from present names: G. K. Chesterton (author/apologist) and Joseph Henry Chesterton (plant collector).

ADD_OR_RESTORE:
- Add an explicit instruction to request the lead-sentence occupation labels (primary vocation(s)) and birth–death years for Henry Green, phrased to support direct comparison of job diversity (e.g., "one-line: name (real name; dates) — primary occupation(s)").
- Add bridge clues and aliases to prefer exact-entity matches: "Henry Green = Henry Vincent Yorke", "Henry Vincent Yorke biography lead", "Henry Green (novelist) infobox occupations", "Henry Green Party Going author".
- Add a short disambiguation step: prefer results that include the pen-name mapping in the lead (not just a disambiguation list) and that list occupations as noun labels (novelist; journalist; civil servant, etc.).
- Add BM25-friendly tokens to include in the retrieval: pen name, real name, novelist, biography lead, infobox, 1905–1973 (as an anchor date cue), "Henry Green (novelist)".

REMOVE_OR_AVOID:
- Remove broad, multi-entity occupation searches that could return long lists (avoid queries that ask for exhaustive career histories at this step).
- Avoid disambiguation-list pages and results dominated by other "Henry" individuals.
- Avoid material about Joseph Henry Chesterton (botanical/19th-century) and other similarly named but irrelevant persons.
- Avoid noisy topically unrelated Henrys (e.g., Henry James) and generic biography summaries that lack a clear pen-name mapping.

QUERY_SHAPE:
Use an exact-entity identity + lead-sentence shape that prioritizes pen-name mapping and primary-occupation nouns. Example instruction form (behavioral, not final query): produce an identity-focused retrieval shaped like
- "Exact match: 'Henry Green' = 'Henry Vincent Yorke' OR 'Henry Vincent Yorke biography lead'"
- Target documents with an infobox/lead line that contains: [Henry Green (born Henry Vincent Yorke; YYYY–YYYY) — primary occupation(s): novelist, ...]
Include tokens: pen name, real name, novelist, infobox, biography lead, life dates.

WHY_THIS_MOVES_RETRIEVAL:
Shifts the retrieval from a narrow identity-confirmation step to one that returns a compact, explicit identity+occupation lead (pen-name mapping + primary role nouns + dates). That produces the precise, BM25-salient phrases needed to judge job diversity while filtering out disambiguation noise and unrelated Henrys. [[ ## completed ## ]]

TRIGGER:
Step 2 — Henry Green (pen name Henry Vincent Yorke) identity and occupations are missing from the local context; retrieval should pivot from a single ambiguous "Henry" cue toward exact-entity occupation leads for both Henry Green and G. K. Chesterton while explicitly disambiguating Joseph Henry Chesterton.

KEEP:
- Preserve the intent to obtain explicit occupation lists to compare job diversity.
- Keep the disambiguation warning: avoid conflating Joseph Henry Chesterton (plant collector) with Henry Green.
- Retain the preference for short encyclopedic lead/infobox evidence (occupation tokens, brief role summaries).
- Maintain local ordering: first secure Henry Green identity/occupations, then fetch Chesterton occupations/disambiguation.

ADD_OR_RESTORE:
- Anchor alias mapping: "Henry Green = Henry Vincent Yorke" (include both tokens in queries).
- Add work/title anchors: "Party Going" (to bias toward the novelist Henry Green).
- Add occupation tokens to boost BM25 overlap: "novelist", "journalist", "civil servant", "editor", "author", "critic".
- Add Chesterton anchors for comparison: "G. K. Chesterton", "Orthodoxy 1908", "Heretics", "author", "apologist".
- Explicit disambiguation cue: "Joseph Henry Chesterton plant collector" as a negative-match anchor to steer away from that person.
- Expected evidence phrasing tokens: "infobox", "lead", "occupations", "born", "died", "career".
- Restore short exact-entity bridge clues: "Henry Vincent Yorke biography", "Henry Green (novelist) occupations", "G. K. Chesterton occupations biography".

REMOVE_OR_AVOID:
- Avoid broad or noisy 'Henry' queries and generic disambiguation pages without occupation/infobox tokens.
- Filter out unrelated Henry hits that dilute BM25 signal: "Henry James", "Henry F. Pringle", "Bill Henry", generic list pages.
- Avoid pages that mention only a single role without an occupations list (e.g., brief mentions, index entries) unless they include an infobox/lead.
- De-emphasize results that prioritize Joseph Henry Chesterton over Henry Green.

QUERY_SHAPE:
- Use short, entity-centric shapes with alias and occupation tokens (behavioral templates, not final strings), in this order:
  1) Exact-entity + alias + occupation cue: [ "Henry Green" OR "Henry Vincent Yorke" ] + (infobox OR lead) + (occupations OR "career" OR "novelist" OR "civil servant" OR "journalist")
  2) Work-anchor verification: [ "Party Going" ] + ("Henry Green" OR "Henry Vincent Yorke") + (lead OR infobox)
  3) Comparator fetch: "G. K. Chesterton" + (infobox OR lead) + (occupations OR "Orthodoxy" OR "Heretics")
  4) Disambiguation negative-check: ("Joseph Henry Chesterton" + "plant collector") as a separate short lookup to confirm distinct identity
- Prefer pages whose leads/infoboxes contain enumerated occupations and short career notes; deprioritize broad disambiguation lists and single-role mentions.

WHY_THIS_MOVES_RETRIEVAL:
Including the pen-name alias and work-title tokens increases BM25 token overlap with the novelist Henry Green's entity page and its infobox lead; adding explicit occupation keywords steers results toward pages that list multiple roles. Pulling Chesterton occupation tokens (Orthodoxy, Heretics, infobox) in parallel ensures both entities return compact occupation lists for direct diversity comparison. Explicitly marking Joseph Henry Chesterton as a negative-match reduces name-confusion noise and prevents false positives that would derail the comparison. This focused, ordered query-shape raises the chance of retrieving concise occupation lists rather than broad or ambiguous Henry results.

max_info.why_largest: This transition makes the biggest shift in retrieval focus: it moves from a noisy, multi-target, ambiguous query (looking for both Henry Green and a full list for G. K. Chesterton, with many unrelated Henrys returned) to a tightly scoped identity-confirmation step for Henry Green (pen name mapping to Henry Vincent Yorke). That resolves the critical alias/surface-form ambiguity and changes the expected evidence family (from broad multi-entity results to a single-entity lead/infobox), substantially reducing noise and rerouting the next retrievals.

pgrads.pgrad:
TRIGGER:
Current multi-target query returns many unrelated "Henry" hits or pages about other Chestertons (e.g., Henry James, Nick Tosches, Joseph Henry Chesterton plant‑collector) and does not surface a clear biographical lead for "Henry Green" (the novelist).

KEEP:
- Anchor G. K. Chesterton / Orthodoxy (1908) as the comparison endpoint (writer, apologist).
- Anchor Joseph Henry Chesterton / plant collector (c.1840–1883) as a disambiguation risk to avoid conflation.
- Preserve intent to gather short encyclopedic biography leads that list occupations/roles and dates.

ADD_OR_RESTORE:
- Add strong entity-exact targets and known aliases: "Henry Green (novelist)", "Henry Vincent Yorke" (pen name), known work token "Party Going".
- Add type and slot cues: biography lead, occupations, career summary, dates (e.g., 1905–1973).
- Add disambiguation-signal: "disambiguation" + title-matching to separate multiple Henry Greens.
- Add site-/lead-priority hints: encyclopedia/Wikipedia-style lead paragraphs or short biographic summaries (infobox/lede).
- Add query intent tokens: novelist, pen name, novelist biography, notable works.

REMOVE_OR_AVOID:
- Deprioritize or filter out results dominated by other Henrys (Henry James, Henry F. Pringle, Nick Tosches) and unrelated authors.
- Avoid pages focused on botanical collecting or James Veitch & Sons unless title clearly shows Joseph Henry Chesterton (use only for explicit disambiguation checks).
- Remove broad ambiguous "Henry Green" hits unless accompanied by disambiguation or novelist/pen‑name signals.

QUERY_SHAPE:
Switch from a single ambiguous multi-target string to an entity-centered multi-hit shape:
- Hit A (disambiguation check): target page titles with "Henry Green" + "disambiguation" to enumerate candidate persons.
- Hit B (novelist focus): exact-match entity hits for aliases/pen name (title or lead strongly matching "Henry Green (novelist)" OR "Henry Vincent Yorke") with added type cues (biography lead, occupations, "Party Going", dates).
- Hit C (comparison anchor): compact lead/infobox hit for "G. K. Chesterton" + occupation list (writer, journalist, critic, lay theologian) to permit direct role-counting.
- Optional filter: deprioritize hits whose titles match botanical collection pages unless used only to confirm Joseph Henry Chesterton identity.

WHY_THIS_MOVES_RETRIEVAL:
Making the queries entity-exact and splitting into disambiguation + alias + comparison hits increases BM25 signal by boosting tokens that uniquely identify the novelist (pen name, key work, life dates) and suppresses high-frequency noisy Henry tokens. The disambiguation step isolates candidate persons, the alias/works tokens pull biography leads that list occupations, and the Chesterton anchor ensures comparable occupation lists—together shifting results from broad, noisy Henry matches toward concentrated biography pages needed to compare job diversity.

TRIGGER:
Disambiguation hit for "Henry Green" with multiple Henrys present and need to confirm the novelist's identity/pen-name for a direct role label (shift from multi-candidate narrowing to exact-identity confirmation).

KEEP:
Retain attention to name-confusion risks (G. K. Chesterton vs. Joseph Henry Chesterton anchors) and keep the objective of finding short encyclopedic leads; preserve the comparative anchor pointing to G. K. Chesterton as a known author/apologist.

ADD_OR_RESTORE:
Add an identity-focused retrieval bias: prioritize pen-name alias mapping and one-line lead/infobox snippets that contain the real name, life dates, and primary vocation. Use high-BM25 tokens together (e.g., "Henry Green" + "Henry Vincent Yorke" + "1905 1973" + "novelist" + "Party Going") and target sources likely to yield lead paragraphs (Wikipedia lead, Britannica/Oxford DNB, short encyclopedia bios). Prefer hits whose title or first sentence contains the alias mapping (pen name = real name) and a concise occupation label.

REMOVE_OR_AVOID:
Deprioritize broad disambiguation pages and generic "Henry" results, long multi-person lists, and documents about Joseph Henry Chesterton (botanical collector) or other unrelated Henrys. Avoid wide occupation-search queries that return exhaustive CVs—focus away from multi-occupation aggregation noise.

QUERY_SHAPE:
Switch from a multi-hit entity-disambiguation shape to an exact-entity identity shape: a compact, anchored conjunction of pen-name + real name + date tokens + a prominent work/title or "novelist" type cue that matches lead/infobox text. Aim for results whose title/lead contains the alias mapping and the one-line role/date snippet (short, high-precision lead retrieval).

WHY_THIS_MOVES_RETRIEVAL:
Concentrating on pen-name alias tokens plus dates and a flagship work promotes lead/infobox matches and suppresses disambiguation and other Henry noise; a confirmed alias + lead-sentence label yields the primary-role mapping (novelist, 1905–1973) needed to compare job diversity against G. K. Chesterton without fetching long, noisy occupation lists.

TRIGGER:
After confirming the pen name mapping but still lacking Henry Green’s occupations/dates — identity known (Henry Green = Henry Vincent Yorke) but no concise lead/infobox line listing roles and life dates to compare job diversity with G. K. Chesterton.

KEEP:
- Preserve the identity-confirmation anchor: "Henry Green" ↔ "Henry Vincent Yorke".
- Keep disambiguation awareness: avoid conflating with Joseph Henry Chesterton.
- Keep aim for a short encyclopedic lead/infobox style result (one-line biography/lead).
- Maintain BM25 anchors already used: "Henry Green", "Henry Vincent Yorke", "novelist", "Party Going".

ADD_OR_RESTORE:
- Expand retrieval focus from mere identity to explicit primary occupations and life dates (e.g., novelist; additional roles such as journalist, civil servant, editor) stated in a lead/infobox line.
- Add notable-work cue ("Party Going") as an anchor to boost precision for the novelist Henry Green.
- Add type cues: "lead paragraph", "infobox occupations", "born 1905", "died 1973" (or life-date tokens) to surface concise biographical summaries.
- Restore a brief disambiguation check phrase limited to confirming this Henry Green is the novelist (to filter other Henrys) — not entire disambiguation pages.
- Prefer sources that present occupation lists in the first sentence or infobox.

REMOVE_OR_AVOID:
- Remove the previous strict limitation that only a single primary-role confirmation is acceptable — allow a short occupations list.
- Avoid broad disambiguation pages and long lists of unrelated Henrys.
- Avoid 19th-century botanical/plant-collector results (Joseph Henry Chesterton) and other noisy Henrys.
- Avoid deep, multi-paragraph career histories at this step — only pull compact lead/infobox lines.

QUERY_SHAPE:
Use an exact-entity + occupation-list lead shape: require the pen-name alias plus life-date/occupation tokens and a lead/infobox cue. Example shape elements (combine, not a final query): 
- quoted alias match ["Henry Green" OR "Henry Vincent Yorke"] + 
- type cue ["lead paragraph" OR "infobox" OR "biography lead"] + 
- occupation tokens [novelist, journalist, civil servant, editor, author] + 
- life-date tokens [1905, 1973, born, died] + 
- work-anchor ["Party Going"] 
AND suppress tokens/sections like ["disambiguation", "Joseph Henry Chesterton", "orchid", "Veitch"].

WHY_THIS_MOVES_RETRIEVAL:
Shifts retrieval from identity-only evidence to a concise biographical lead that explicitly lists occupations and dates. The pen-name + life-date + notable-work anchors raise BM25 relevance for the novelist Henry Green and the infobox/lead cue surfaces a compact occupations list needed to judge job diversity while the suppression tokens reduce distracting Henrys and Joseph Henry Chesterton results.

TRIGGER:
Step 3 local prompt update to shift retrieval from the ambiguous R_left state (missing Henry Green) toward the target R_right that lists explicit occupations for both Henry Green (Henry Vincent Yorke) and G. K. Chesterton for a jobs-diversity comparison.

KEEP:
- Preserve the entity-focus: "Henry Green — pen name of Henry Vincent Yorke".
- Preserve the goal to obtain an explicit occupations list (novelist, journalist, civil servant, etc.) and short dates to assess diversity.
- Preserve the disambiguation precaution: avoid conflating Henry Green with other Henrys and Joseph Henry Chesterton.
- Preserve expected evidence type: concise encyclopedic lead/infobox or biography lead lines that enumerate occupations.

ADD_OR_RESTORE:
- Add explicit anchor phrases and aliases to raise BM25 signal: "Henry Vincent Yorke", "Henry Green (novelist)", "Party Going" (title cue), plus birth–death years as anchors (e.g., 1905–1973) to narrow entity hits.
- Add parallel anchor for comparator: "G. K. Chesterton", "Gilbert Keith Chesterton", "Orthodoxy (1908)" and Chesterton date anchors (e.g., 1874–1936) to surface infobox/lead occupation lines.
- Restore disambiguation targets to suppress noise: "Henry Green (disambiguation)", "Henry Yorke biography", and an explicit check phrase "Joseph Henry Chesterton plant collector" to confirm different person.
- Prefer sources/phrases that produce compact occupation lists: "infobox occupations", "lead: occupations", "biography occupations" rather than long narrative pages.
- Add type cues to prefer short factual lines: "novelist; journalist; civil servant; editor" and "author; apologist; journalist" as retrieval-weighting anchors (not assertions).

REMOVE_OR_AVOID:
- Deprioritize broad or noisy "Henry" queries and disambiguation pages that list many unrelated Henrys (e.g., Henry James, Bill Henry, Henry F. Pringle).
- Avoid queries that match "Joseph Henry Chesterton" without the plant-collector disambiguator.
- Avoid broad multi-entity comparative queries (e.g., "Chesterton vs Green careers") at this step — keep single-entity, infobox-style retrievals.
- Avoid result types that are lists/disambiguation only without lead/infobox context.

QUERY_SHAPE:
- Use exact-entity, short, BM25-friendly shapes that prioritize infobox/lead occupation lines and pen-name mapping, for example:
  - "<Full name / pen name> biography" with alias included (behavioural template: include both forms: 'Henry Vincent Yorke' + 'Henry Green (novelist)').
  - "<Name> infobox" / "<Name> occupations" / " <Name> biography lead".
  - "Joseph Henry Chesterton plant collector" as a disambiguation-check shape.
- Prefer single-entity queries per request, include title cues (book titles, dates) to bump correct entity documents.

WHY_THIS_MOVES_RETRIEVAL:
Focusing queries on exact-entity + alias + infobox/lead occupation lines (and adding title/date anchors like Party Going, Orthodoxy, and birth–death years) increases BM25 relevance for the correct Henry Green page and for G. K. Chesterton’s occupation list while filtering out unrelated Henrys and the Joseph Henry Chesterton noise. This yields compact occupation enumerations needed to compare job diversity without pulling broad or multi-entity result sets.

max_info.why_largest: This segment moves the retrieval from simple pen-name disambiguation toward actively resolving the alias and retrieving explicit occupation lists: it closes the missing-entity/alias gap (Henry Green = Henry Vincent Yorke), shifts the expected evidence from a short identity line to full biographical/occupation evidence, and increases answerability by preparing to fetch Chesterton's occupations for direct comparison.
